era_context: The early 16th century is marked by the Protestant Reformation's consolidation in Northern Europe, Ottoman expansion under Süleyman the Magnificent, the Spanish conquest of the Americas accelerating, and the Ming Dynasty in China under the Jiajing Emperor. The year 1526 falls during a period of intense religious and political upheaval in Europe, with the Reformation spreading despite papal and imperial opposition. In Asia, the Ottoman Empire is expanding aggressively into Eastern Europe and the Mediterranean, while the Mughal Empire is being established in India.

events.title: Battle of Mohács: Ottoman victory over Hungary
events.description: On August 29, 1526, Ottoman forces under Süleyman the Magnificent defeated the Hungarian army at the Battle of Mohács, one of the most significant military engagements in European history. The Hungarian army, led by King Louis II, was decisively defeated and largely destroyed. King Louis II was killed in the battle (or drowned while fleeing), and the Hungarian kingdom was effectively destroyed as an independent power. The Ottoman victory opened the way for Ottoman expansion into Central Europe and the Balkans. Following the battle, Ottoman forces occupied much of Hungary, and the region was divided: the Ottoman Empire controlled the central plains, while the northern and western parts came under Habsburg control. The battle marked a turning point in European history: it demonstrated Ottoman military superiority and initiated a period of Ottoman dominance in Eastern Europe that would last until the late 17th century. The battle also had profound consequences for Hungary: the kingdom was partitioned, and Hungarian independence was lost for centuries.
key_figures: Louis II (King of Hungary, killed in the battle)
Ferdinand I (Archduke of Austria, Charles V's brother, who claimed the Hungarian throne after Louis II's death)

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles and court records (Peçevi, Solakzade)
Hungarian and Austrian sources and diplomatic records
Contemporary accounts by eyewitnesses and participants

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Mohács is extensively documented in both Ottoman and European sources. The date (August 29, 1526), the outcome, and the key figures are well-established. The death of King Louis II is confirmed in contemporary sources, though some details vary.
cross_references: Precedes 1529-001 (Siege of Vienna, enabled by Ottoman victory at Mohács)

events.title: Battle of Panipat: Babur defeats Ibrahim Lodi and establishes Mughal Empire
events.description: On April 21, 1526, Babur, a Central Asian conqueror descended from Timur and Genghis Khan, defeated Ibrahim Lodi, the Sultan of Delhi, at the First Battle of Panipat. Babur's victory was decisive: he killed Ibrahim Lodi and defeated the Delhi Sultanate's army, establishing Mughal control over North India. The battle is considered one of the most significant military engagements in Indian history, as it marked the beginning of the Mughal Empire, which would dominate India for the next two centuries. Babur's victory was aided by superior military tactics, artillery, and the use of gunpowder weapons, which gave the Mughals an advantage over traditional Indian cavalry. The battle marked a turning point in Indian history: the Mughal Empire would become one of the largest and most powerful empires in the world, and Indian history would be shaped by Mughal rule for centuries. Babur's conquest was rapid and thorough: within a few years, he had consolidated control over much of North India.
key_figures: Babur (Central Asian conqueror, founder of the Mughal Empire)
Ibrahim Lodi (Sultan of Delhi, defeated and killed)
Rana Sanga (Rajput ruler, who would challenge Babur at Khanwa in 1527)

sources.name: Babur's own account in the 'Baburnama' (Memoirs of Babur)
Contemporary Mughal chronicles and court records
Later Indian historical accounts and chronicles

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Panipat is well-documented in Mughal sources, particularly Babur's own memoirs. The date (April 21, 1526) and the outcome are well-established, though some details of the battle vary between sources.
cross_references: Precedes 1527-002 (Battle of Khanwa, Babur's subsequent victory)
Precedes 1530-001 (Babur's death and succession of Humayun)

events.title: Diet of Speyer (First Diet) addresses religious crisis
events.description: The First Diet of Speyer convened in 1526 to address the religious crisis triggered by Martin Luther's Reformation. The Diet was called by Charles V (Holy Roman Emperor) to deal with the spread of Protestantism and to coordinate imperial and princely responses. The Diet's outcome was significant: it granted German princes greater autonomy in religious matters within their territories, effectively permitting the spread of Protestantism in territories whose rulers favored it. This decision strengthened princely power at the expense of both papal and imperial authority. The Diet of Speyer in 1526 established a precedent that would be formalized at the Second Diet of Speyer in 1529 and ultimately institutionalized in the Peace of Augsburg (1555). The Diet's outcome reflected the reality that Charles V lacked the power to suppress Protestantism by force and had to accommodate princely demands for religious autonomy.
key_figures: Ferdinand I (Archduke of Austria, Charles's brother)

sources.name: Official Diet records and imperial decrees
Correspondence of German princes and imperial officials

events.certainty_note: The First Diet of Speyer in 1526 is well-documented in imperial and princely archives. The Diet's proceedings and decrees are well-established, though the interpretation of their significance varies among historians.

events.title: Mughal Empire established in India; Babur crowned emperor
events.description: Following his victory at the Battle of Panipat (April 21, 1526), Babur established the Mughal Empire and crowned himself emperor. The Mughal Empire was founded on the basis of Babur's military conquest and his claim to be the heir of Timur and Genghis Khan. Babur's establishment of the Mughal Empire marked the beginning of a new era in Indian history: the Mughal Empire would become one of the largest and most powerful empires in the world, and Indian history would be shaped by Mughal rule for the next two centuries. The Mughal Empire was characterized by a synthesis of Central Asian, Persian, and Indian cultures, and it would become known for its architectural achievements (including the Taj Mahal), its administrative systems, and its cultural refinement. Babur's reign was relatively short (he died in 1530), but he established the foundations of the Mughal Empire that would be built upon by his successors.
key_figures: Babur (founder and first emperor of the Mughal Empire)
Rana Sanga (Rajput ruler, rival to Babur)

sources.name: Babur's own account in the 'Baburnama' (Memoirs of Babur)
Contemporary Mughal chronicles and court records
Later Indian historical accounts and chronicles

events.certainty_note: The establishment of the Mughal Empire and Babur's coronation are well-documented in Mughal sources. The date (1526, following the Battle of Panipat in April) is well-established.
cross_references: Precedes 1530-001 (Babur's death and succession of Humayun)

events.title: Treaty of Madrid between Charles V and Francis I
events.description: In January 1526, the Treaty of Madrid was signed between Charles V (Holy Roman Emperor) and Francis I (King of France), ending the war between the two powers. The treaty was negotiated after Charles V's victory at the Battle of Pavia (1525), where Francis I was captured. The Treaty of Madrid required Francis I to renounce his claims to Italian territories and to pay a large ransom for his release. However, Francis I repudiated the treaty shortly after his release, claiming that it had been signed under duress. The treaty's failure to establish lasting peace between Charles V and Francis I reflected the fundamental rivalry between the two powers for dominance in Europe. The treaty's failure also had consequences for the papacy: the Pope, fearing Charles V's power, sought alliances with France, which contributed to the Sack of Rome in 1527. The Treaty of Madrid marked a moment of apparent Habsburg triumph, but the underlying conflicts between Charles V and Francis I would continue for decades.
key_figures: Pope Clement VII (interested party in the treaty's outcome)

sources.name: Spanish and French royal records and chronicles
Contemporary accounts by diplomats and chroniclers

events.certainty_note: The Treaty of Madrid in January 1526 is well-documented in Spanish, French, and diplomatic sources. The treaty's terms and Francis I's subsequent repudiation are well-established.
cross_references: Follows from 1525-001 (Battle of Pavia, which led to the treaty)
Precedes 1527-001 (Sack of Rome, partly a consequence of the treaty's failure)

events.title: Portuguese establish trading post in Goa; consolidation of Portuguese India
events.description: In 1510, Portuguese forces under Afonso de Albuquerque had captured Goa from the Bijapur Sultanate. By 1526, Goa had been firmly established as the capital of the Portuguese Estado da Índia (Portuguese State of India) and the administrative and commercial hub of Portuguese operations in Asia. The year 1526 marks a point in the consolidation of Portuguese control: Goa had become a fortified settlement with a cathedral, administrative apparatus, and a growing Portuguese population. The establishment of Goa as the center of Portuguese India was significant: it served as the base for Portuguese expansion throughout the Indian Ocean and Southeast Asia, and it represented the beginning of European colonial dominance in Asia. The Portuguese presence in Goa would last until 1961, when India reclaimed the territory. NOTE: The actual conquest of Goa occurred in 1510; 1526 represents a consolidation point.
key_figures: Afonso de Albuquerque (Portuguese military commander, conquest in 1510)
Vasco da Gama (Portuguese explorer, earlier arrival in India 1498)

sources.name: Contemporary accounts by Portuguese merchants and officials

events.certainty_note: The Portuguese conquest of Goa in 1510 is well-documented. The 1526 date marks a consolidation point in the establishment of Portuguese control, documented in Portuguese administrative records.
cross_references: Contemporary with 1526-002 (Battle of Panipat, which established Mughal Empire in India)

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Mohács (1526-001) is sometimes portrayed as a decisive turning point that marked the end of Hungarian independence, but modern scholarship notes that Hungarian resistance continued for decades and that Ottoman control was contested. The narrative of Ottoman invincibility is also complicated by the fact that the Ottomans failed to capture Vienna in 1529 and that their expansion eventually slowed. The Battle of Panipat (1526-002) is sometimes presented as a decisive victory that established Mughal dominance, but modern scholarship notes that Mughal control remained contested and that Hindu and Afghan resistance continued for decades. The characterization of Babur as the founder of a unified Mughal Empire is somewhat anachronistic: the empire was still being consolidated, and it took decades for the Mughals to establish firm control over India. The Treaty of Madrid (1526-005) is sometimes presented as a triumph for Charles V, but its failure to establish lasting peace and its role in provoking the Sack of Rome suggest that it was less successful than initially appeared. The Portuguese establishment in Goa (1526-006) is sometimes presented as the beginning of European colonial dominance in Asia, but the reality was more complex: Portuguese control was limited to coastal trading posts, and indigenous powers remained dominant in the interior.
historiographic_note: The year 1526 is well-documented in European and Asian sources due to the prevalence of written records and administrative archives. The Battle of Mohács is documented in both Ottoman and European sources, though the perspectives differ. The Battle of Panipat is documented in Mughal sources, particularly Babur's own memoirs, but indigenous Indian perspectives are mediated through these elite sources. The Diet of Speyer is documented in imperial and princely archives. The Treaty of Madrid is documented in Spanish, French, and diplomatic sources. The Portuguese establishment in Goa is documented in Portuguese administrative records. However, the perspective is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; the experiences of common people, women, and non-elite groups are largely absent. The historiography of 1526 has been shaped by later narratives of Ottoman expansion, the rise of the Mughal Empire, European expansion, and the Reformation, which may distort the significance of events as they were understood at the time. African and Southeast Asian sources for this year are sparse in European archives, reflecting colonial-era biases in what was preserved and studied.

graph_edges.note: The Ottoman victory at Mohács (1526) enabled the consolidation of Ottoman control over Hungary (1528).
The Ottoman victory at Mohács (1526) enabled the Siege of Vienna (1529).
Babur's victory at Panipat (1526) was followed by his victory at Khanwa (1527).
The Battle of Panipat (1526) led to the establishment of the Mughal Empire (1526).
The First Diet of Speyer (1526) was a response to the Edict of Worms (1521).
The First Diet of Speyer (1526) was followed by the Second Diet of Speyer (1529).
The Treaty of Madrid (1526) was a consequence of the Battle of Pavia (1525).
The failure of the Treaty of Madrid (1526) contributed to the Sack of Rome (1527).
The consolidation of Portuguese control in Goa (1526) followed the conquest (1510).

The Battle of Panipat and the establishment of the Mughal Empire (1526) occurred simultaneously with the consolidation of Portuguese control in Goa (1526), illustrating divergent trajectories in Asia.
